data.title: Inter & Co Q1: Lower Risk Products Deliver Results
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying shares of Inter & Co (INTR) after the release of the Q1 2025 results. This article is a continuation of my coverage initiation article published on April 20, 2024, with the same recommendation.

In this article, I will provide a new briefing on the company's history, but the main focus is a detailed analysis of the company's published results and linking them to my investment thesis.

Corporate Profile

Inter & Co was created in 1994 under the name Intermedium Financeira, and at the time, it belonged to MRV, a construction company listed on the Brazilian stock exchange B3 (OTCPK:BOLSY).

In 2015, now called Banco Intermedium, the company grew significantly alongside Nu (NU), offering disruptive products and services in the traditional and bureaucratic Brazilian banking market.

Today, the company is a digital bank that is experiencing strong growth. The number of customers has surpassed 37 million, gross revenues exceed BRL 3 billion ($559 million), and the credit portfolio already exceeds BRL 42 billion ($7.44 billion).

Review of Inter & Co's Results

Inter & Co released its Q1 results on May 12, and as we can see below, earnings per share came in within market expectations, and shares rose 5.2% on the day.

Below, I will provide a detailed description of the company's results, with critical analysis and perspectives. The company is Brazilian, and although it is listed on the Nasdaq, it publishes its results in Brazilian currency. Therefore, I will convert the results using the exchange rate 1 USD = 5.72 BRL, as this was the exchange rate on the last day of the quarter.

Credit Portfolio

Inter's credit portfolio rose 5% q/q and 21% y/y to BRL 37.4 billion ($6.54 billion). Highlights include the FGTS guaranteed loan, which grew 43% y/y, and home equity, which grew 45% y/y, as we can see below.

The bank will continue to focus on products with risk-adjusted returns. In my view, the private payroll loan product also seems like a good future driver, due to the bank's scalable and digital business model.

Cost of Credit and NPL

Here, we had another great result, in my opinion. NPL>90 days fell 15 bps q/q and 76 bps y/y to 4.1%, while short-term NPL rose 40 bps q/q to 3.8%, however, short-term NPL was impacted by seasonal effects, as occurred in the results of Itaú (ITUB) and Bradesco (BBD).

Credit quality is healthy, and the improvement trajectory should continue, in my view. The bank has shown a lot of pragmatism over the last few quarters and should focus on quality.

Revenues

The company's net interest income rose 8.3% q/q and 37.3% y/y to BRL 1.36 billion ($238 million). The reasons for this increase were a better product mix, asset repricing, and customer segmentation.

In my view, net interest income should remain strong due to new allocations and a better product mix. Next, we will discuss expenses before analyzing the net profit from the operation.

Expenses

The company's expenses changed -1% q/q and +32% y/y to BRL 831 million ($145 million). It is worth noting that the increase was lower than the growth in interest income, additionally, these were investments that I consider strategic, such as technology and marketing.

The efficiency gain trends should continue. Additionally, the business model is very interesting, as the increase in the customer base will help dilute costs and expenses. It is worth noting that the number of customers increased by 1.6 million in just one quarter to 37.7 million customers.

Net Income

The growth in the credit portfolio, net interest income, and expense control had an effect on the bottom line. Net income increased 4.1% q/q and 56.8% y/y to BRL 287 million ($50.2 million), while ROE rose to 12.8%.

Every quarter I see that the bank is closer to achieving its 60/30/30 plan, 60 million customers, 30% efficiency, and 30% ROE by 2027. My assessment is that the results were good, and now let's talk about valuation.

Valuation

Inter & Co has a very different business model from any other bank, so we will use the P/E multiple based on the company's own history. Let's look at the history below.

The company has traded between 8x and 17x earnings over the past year, with an average of 12.5x earnings. The company is currently trading at 10.8x earnings, a conservative upside of 15.7%, which supports the buy recommendation.

Additionally, Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend a strong buy on the stock, which makes me even more confident in my recommendation.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

As I mentioned in the credit portfolio segment, one of the pillars of the future thesis is anchored in the private payroll loan product. If expectations for this new product are frustrated, this could mean a drop in shares.

Another risk is that one of the contributors to the growth in net interest income was the migration of fee income to this segment. This was due to regulatory changes.

If the bank is unable to maintain the strong results from this segment in the future, expectations could again be disappointed, and the share price could fall.

The Bottom Line

Inter & Co delivered strong results across all segments. The strategy of focusing on lower-risk products has delivered results and is very welcome in a macroeconomic environment as complex as Brazil's.

Revenues and credit portfolio have expanded healthily, and long-term delinquency is on a downward trajectory. Additionally, the valuation indicates a conservative upside of 15.7%.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy Inter & Co shares. In my view, the digital bank is firmly pursuing its goal of 60 million customers, 30% efficiency, and 30% ROE by 2027.

data.title: Oracle: Cloud Inflection Priced In; Initiate At Neutral
data.description:
Summary

We initiate coverage on Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) with Neutral and $145 PT. Oracle Corp. offers a suite of cloud, software, and infrastructure products globally to the enterprise. Our Neutral is based on our near-term estimates of EPS (Dil): $5.96 in FY25E (+7.2% y/y vs. Street $5.97), before accelerating to $7.25 in FY26E (+21.6% y/y vs Street $6.65), driven by easing supply constraints and the conversion of ORCL's $130B remaining performance obligations to cloud revenues.

We value ORCL based on 30.0x FY26E PE, which is a factor of 25% cloud rev growth, AI leverage in R&D ($8.9B) and Capex ($12.1B), and underappreciated spring-loaded margin expansion in FY26, while factoring in ORCL's ongoing margin deleverage in the current quarter from elevated cloud infrastructure buildout. Management's mid-teens rev guide and Project Stargate give visibility to the accelerating earnings, while supply constraints and outsized capex are drags in the near-term. Our $145 PT, implies limited TSR as we feel the spring-loaded FY26 inflection is largely baked in at current levels. We view this as balanced risk/reward and rate ORCL Neutral.

Executing for a Big FY26

We model FY25E EPS (Dil) of $5.96 (+7.2% Y/Y), slightly below Street consensus ($5.97, +11.4%) and an above-consensus FY26E of $7.25 (+21.6% Y/Y vs. Street $6.65, +11.4%). The near term conservatism is driven by guidance and modest EPS misses over Q2 and 3, as well as significant near term capex needed to support high velocity cloud deployments (which constrains profitability near term). Critically, management has indicated that supply constraints continue to limit OCI revenue recognition and margin leverage, with full impact of unprecedented RPO backlog ($130B) and data center buildout only flowing through in FY26.

Source : FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $57.08B 21 $65.09B 27
EBITDA $24.31B 21 $27.72B 27
Net Income $16.86B 21 $18.85B 27
EPS $5.97 24 $6.65 21
SG&A Expenses $12.25B 21 $13.96B 27

In contrast, the bull case for FY26 is supported by multiple high-visibility catalysts: management's explicit guidance for 15-16% revenue growth, acceleration in OCI (as it could easily exceed FY25 growth rate of 50%+), rapid RPO conversion as supply grid expands, and commercializing transformational AI initiatives (Project Stargate) that is not fully reflected in RPO or Street models. We believe consensus underestimates the magnitude and velocity of these fundamental changes, and the margin leverage they result in, giving us conviction that our above-consensus FY26 EPS view is best positioned to capture Oracle's spring-loaded growth opportunity.

Cloud Earnings Inflection

The earnings inflection to FY26 is not a mere hope but is backed by unprecedented forward RPO visibility, which in turn is a remarkable event in the history of enterprise tech: RPO has exploded to $130B (63% YoY growth) on the back of $48B+ of net new RPO signed in one quarter, of which cloud RPO grew 90%+. This is no mere pipeline: Management expects it to drive 15% rev growth in FY26, with an even steeper slope for cloud infra as new capacity comes online and supply constraints ease.

And the inflection is not even fully loaded yet: the Stargate AI project (potential $100B catalyst) is not in the numbers and is set to turbocharge the reacceleration off the starting gate. The scale of the AI backlog and the structural increase in RPO from longer and larger cloud deals create a rare springboard for EPS growth and margin expansion as capital intensity normalizes. We see the combination of these factors — record bookings, unprecedented revenue backlog, and untapped AI catalysts — as making the FY26 reacceleration both highly visible and structurally supported: a very rare combination in large-cap tech.

Valuation

Our $145 PT based on 30.0x fwd P/E multiple to our FY26E EPS of $4.84 implies -10% downside, supporting our Neutral Rating. We believe the multiple incorporates the accelerating momentum that Oracle is seeing in Cloud, backed by 25% Cloud Rev growth and $130B of RPO, and the inflection in FY26 on the earnings side as the heavy investment in AI and Cloud infrastructure should be converted into earnings power. Our PT multiple is at the 82nd percentile against ORCL's 10-year history (median: 26.6x), which we view as justified given management's explicit FY26 guidance for mid-teens rev growth and our above-consensus 21.6% EPS growth expectation for FY26 as supply chain pressures ease.

Relative to peers, ORCL still trades at a significant discount (80th percentile, 38.0x current vs 100.6x peer median) despite consensus still discounting the pace at which ORCL's massive RPO backlog can convert to earnings power once the platform constraints ease. Also, while Street focus is on margin pressures from outsized spend, we think the scale and deliberate timing of capital deployment sets up for a margin snap-back with the cadence of cloud profitability still underappreciated. Overall, we think this strikes a fair balance between the short-term operational challenges vs the spring-loaded earnings power, resulting in a fairly priced risk/reward.

Risks to Our Neutral

The key risk to our Neutral rating on ORCL is upside not fully appreciated from AI cloud strategy driving faster RPO conversion or margin expansion than we model. With aggressive doubling of capex to ramp data center capacity, ORCL could unlock a wave of OCI / multi-cloud SaaS revenue ahead of our model—especially if Project Stargate ramps faster or multi-cloud partnerships drive incremental high-margin growth not yet fully credited by the Street. Also, strategic sunset of legacy GRC suites in favor of Oracle Risk Management Cloud could accelerate SaaS migration & cross-sell dynamics, compressing cloud adoption cycles and driving operating leverage beyond Street expectations.

Finally, our thesis could be disrupted if sustained AI-driven demand results in positive earnings revisions, especially if macro environment remains supportive of enterprise IT spend and ORCL's license/support base proves more resilient than the Street expects (i.e. faster RPO recognition and operating margin snap-back). We are watching quarterly cloud backlog realization rates, OCI revenue inflections, and signs of enterprise customers accelerating adoption or achieving faster compliance transitions as potential indicators of upside risk to our current thesis.

Conclusion

We feel Oracle's compelling FY26 earnings inflection story (with a massive $130B cloud backlog) is now baked in at the current share price, which leaves us Neutral. Our thesis: there's material upside to our +DD EPS growth and margin expansion view as supply chain looseness fades, but the risk/reward feels balanced at our $145 target, which bakes in a lot of the story. We acknowledge upside surprises are possible if OCI adoption accelerates or Project Stargate outperforms, but near-term headwinds from high capex and the conversion of RPO leave us without conviction at current levels. In summary, we suggest patience: smart money should wait for quarter-to-quarter confirmation of cloud execution, backlog conversion, and the trajectory of OCI margins to see if the spring-loaded thesis is starting to materialize and is being mispriced. At these levels, we'd wait for an entry point or if fundamentals start to exceed even our optimistic out-year scenario before making a strong move.

data.title: Flower Foods: Wait For The Turnaround
data.description:
Flowers Foods shares (NYSE:FLO) have been on a steady downward trend since reaching an all-time high in November 2022. The shares are now down more than 50%.

The company manufactures and distributes packaged baked goods under brands such as Nature's Own, Dave's Killer Bread, Canyon Bakehouse, and Tastykake.

Investment thesis

With an adjusted P/E ratio of 13.2, Flowers Food currently appears to be attractively valued, especially when we look at historical multiples, which were often around 19.

However, Flowers Food has a growth problem that affects both its top and bottom lines. In the current market environment, I see little opportunity for Flowers Foods to regain its growth momentum. I also take a rather critical view of the expensive acquisition of Simple Mills for $795 million. The deal was probably only worthwhile for the previous owner of Simple Mills, but less so for Flowers Foods and its shareholders.

The dividend yield of 5.7% may be interesting for income investors, but given the high payout ratio and little room for further increases, I don't view the yield as a compelling buy argument.

Furthermore, I do not see any significant upside potential at present. The downward trend in the share price is very stable, and there is no catalyst for a sudden reversal. In such an environment, a purchase is not compelling. Investors looking for dividend yields should add the stock to their watch list and keep an eye on it. In my opinion, it makes more sense to wait for a turnaround than to invest in a business with little growth and a less-than-optimal product mix.

Growth momentum and outlook are not good

Flowers Foods is struggling with top-line and bottom-line growth. Adjusted EPS has been stagnating since 2020, and revenue growth has also virtually ground to a halt over the past two years.

Measured against the targets set in 2019 of net sales growth between 1% and 2% and adjusted EPS growth between 7% and 9%, the company is therefore quite a long way off. YoY, sales growth was only 0.25%. Flowers Foods was only able to increase its adjusted diluted EPS by $0.08 to $1.28 in 2024.

Flowers Foods is now attempting to generate inorganic growth through acquisitions. In January, the company announced that it would acquire Simple Mills, a producer of bars, cookies, and crackers, for $795 million and integrate it as an independent brand in the Flowers Foods portfolio. Simple Mills' most recent revenue was $240 million, growing at an impressive average rate of 28% per year over the last five years. Most recently, growth momentum slowed slightly to 14%, although the previous year's figure of 40% was particularly high.

With the acquisition of Simple Mills, Flowers Foods will generate inorganic growth of 5% on company-wide revenue of $5 billion. Without Simple Mills, management expects growth of only 1.5% to 3% for 2025. Conversely, the price for Simple Mills is quite high, at three times last year's revenue. Flowers Foods cannot really afford such an acquisition, as even before the takeover, it had interest-bearing debt of over $1 billion, offset by cash and cash equivalents of only $5 million. Since the Simple Mills acquisition is to be financed solely by debt, total debt is expected to rise to around $1.9 billion. Furthermore (and I credit Flowers Foods for its transparency), Simple Mills' adjusted diluted EPS will be negative and will weigh on earnings by between $0.07 and $0.04.

I take a rather critical view of the acquisition. It is difficult to justify such a premium price for a cracker and cookie manufacturer. In my experience, such brands often go through a hype phase and disappear again quite quickly. The Simple Mills story also shows how low the barriers to market entry are. Simple Mills was literally founded by Katlin Smith in 2012 in her own kitchen with start-up capital of ‘only’ $70,000. Let's take this perspective: As an investor, I would ask myself why Flowers Foods did not come up with the idea of producing and marketing such products itself. With the $795 million acquisition, the company could have launched 100 similar ventures, considering Katlin Smith's start-up capital.

One last word on market share: Flowers Foods is increasingly losing market share. The trend worsened significantly in Q4, leading to a loss of almost one percentage point in the bread segment. Although Q4 is particularly weak (presumably because consumers turn to other manufacturers during the holidays), the sudden drop in the cake segment in particular indicates that this is more of a structural than a seasonal problem.

Update: Q1 numbers are disappointing

The Q1 results for 2025 were also disappointing. Adjusted diluted EPS fell by $0.03 to $0.35. Revenue decreased by 1.4% to $1.554 billion. Inorganic revenue growth from Simple Mills was completely offset by pricing/mix and volume declines.

In addition, Flowers Foods had to lower its forecast for FY 2025. The company now expects revenue to grow only 3.8% to 5.7% instead of 5.9% to 7.5%. At least Simple Mills is driving sales here because, excluding the Simple Mills acquisition, net sales growth is expected to be -0.5% to 1.3% instead of the previous 1.5% to 3.0% growth. But here, too, my above fears are proving to be true because Simple Mills is growing less strongly than originally thought. Management expects Simple Mills to contribute $218 million to $225 million to net sales, compared to prior guidance of $223 million to $230 million. An additional problem is that the forecast for FY 2025 covers 53 weeks. On a 52-week basis, the result would therefore be even worse.

What investors get for their money

Analysts' outlook for the coming years is rather poor. They expect EPS to fall by 9% in FY 2025. Analysts do not anticipate any noticeable improvement in the following years, 2026 and 2027. I expect expectations to deteriorate even further, especially after the disappointing Q1 figures.

Of course, with a forward P/E ratio of 14.51, Flowers Foods appears cheap, especially in a historical context, considering the 5y P/E ratio median north of 25.

Conversely, what is the benefit of a low multiple when the following aspects are taken into account?

The fourth quarter in particular saw a deterioration in market share. Flowers Foods may have to offer higher discounts to combat the loss of market share. This would then have a negative impact on the already low net margin of 4.8%. In addition, the white bread and sweet baked goods categories of Flowers Foods' core business do not fit in with the nutrition-conscious trend that is set to continue in the coming years. The company is therefore struggling with structural headwinds. For a company like this, I do not consider a P/E ratio of 14 to be low. I do not see a margin of safety. The market also seems to agree with me, as the share is in a very stable downward trend.

I am also not convinced by the dividend yield of 5.7%. Although I do not see any immediate risk of a cut, the payout ratio of 80%, the low-margin business, and the difficult market conditions rule out any significant increases in my view. The last dividend increase in May 2024 was 4.3%. I do not expect the next increase to be any higher. This leaves a reasonably secure dividend yield expectation of 5.7% to 6% for the next few years (if we factor in increases). That is acceptable, but it is of no benefit to me if poor share performance more or less eats up the dividend yield. And in the case of Flowers Foods, given the above, I see a rather poor risk/reward ratio with regard to short- and medium-term share price performance.

Conclusion and risks to my thesis

I do not currently see any compelling reason to buy Flowers Foods shares. The stock is only a hold. Investors who have not yet invested in the stock should wait on the sidelines for the downward trend to stabilize or even reverse.

The risk to my thesis here is, of course, that Flowers Foods shares could suddenly jump 10% or even 20% in a very short period of time (for example, due to better-than-expected Q2 results) and then realize a large part of their upside potential. I am personally willing to accept this risk. I act on the principle that I would rather miss out on a rally than enter into an investment with a poor risk/reward ratio and then lose money.

data.title: Devon Sells Matterhorn, What Does This Mean For Midstream
data.description:
WhiteWater Midstream, Enbridge (NYSE:ENB), and MPLX (NYSE:MPLX) have announced that they agreed to acquire Devon Energy's (NYSE:DVN) equity interests in the recently put into service Matterhorn Express pipeline for $375mm; this was a 12.5% equity stake, so it valued the pipeline at $5,700mm in total including project level debt.

Matterhorn is a 2.5 Bcf/d natural gas pipeline, moving natural gas from the Waha hub in the Permian Basin into the Katy hub, a natural gas trading hub near Houston which provides connectivity in all three major directions that natural gas exits Texas: to the Gulf Coast, to Mexico, and to Louisiana. We've talked often about how important gas takeaway has been for the Waha hub in recent years and, even given recent press on potential geology-driven peaks in Permian production out of management teams like Diamondback Energy (FANG), it's clear that Enbridge and MPLX saw a lot of value in adding to their stakes in this pipeline. Given EBITDA estimates of $450mm for Matterhorn on a gross basis, this is a 12.8x EBITDA multiple. Particularly for MPLX but also true for Enbridge, both companies were willing to pay more than the EBITDA multiples both companies trade at in the public markets to acquire more interest in this firm.

For me, that's the first takeaway from this deal. The value of large diameter natural gas pipelines in Texas is not shrinking in the face of commodity price turmoil - and, if anything, may have moved higher lately. Recall that Gulf Coast Express had two transactions in 2024; 10.6x EBITDA (ArcLight buying Phillips 66 (PSX) stake) and 10.4x (Kinetik Holdings (KNTK) also selling its stake to ArcLight). While not the same route, Waha -> Agua Dulce and Waha -> Katy are close enough that I think the comps are pretty darn close. Given that, it's interesting to see the value placed on the Matterhorn today.

Devon Energy had this to say on the Q1 2025 conference call on the transaction:

I would just tell you we're taking a holistic look, that maybe there's sometimes we need to expand our midstream footprint to replicate what we're doing there with the Grayson assets. And I would say there's other times, for example, with Matterhorn, where it's kind of served its purpose. We continue to hold on to that capacity which is incredibly valuable, but the equity ownership clearly wasn't reflected in our organizational value. And therefore, when we monetize, it's just additional cash.

So, number one here, is that Devon Energy did not sell its capacity to ship on the pipeline. That can be common in deals like this, where an anchor shipper that also helped fund the capital to build the pipeline sells both its interest in the earnings of the pipeline but also its contractual rights to ship. So this means that Devon Energy isn't going to see a negative impact on its natural gas price realizations because of this - egress out of Waha is still tight and if it had sold the capacity, it would have had to sell the natural gas for very cheap in the basin.

But it's also an indictment on how some investors think about energy. We see stats like EBITDA per barrel produced, capital expenditures necessary to replace reserves, operating expenses per barrel. All of these metrics, at the minimum, need adjustment for midstream asset ownership. Independent producers rarely get full credit for the value of midstream infrastructure they own, especially true nowadays where the valuation difference between pure play oil and gas producers (4.0 - 5.5x EBITDA) and midstream firms (9.0 - 11.0x EBITDA) is so wide. This sale is a great source of value generation for Devon that will help the company even beyond their recent commitment to cutting a billion dollars in costs across the business, and while it's meaningful, Matterhorn is by no means the entirety of its ownership in pipelines and supporting infrastructure.

Takeaways

The Matterhorn Express sale is a clear example of the hidden value that often exists within upstream energy firms' midstream footprints - value that markets routinely fail to recognize. Devon’s monetization strategy here underscores a smart capital allocation pivot: realizing a premium valuation for a non-core equity stake while retaining the commercial benefit of long term takeaway capacity. It also reinforces that quality midstream infrastructure in Texas - particularly gas egress out of the Permian - is still highly prized by strategic buyers, even at premiums to public trading multiples. As commodity prices remain volatile and consolidation continues across energy subsectors, transactions like this highlight the growing bifurcation in valuation between midstream and upstream companies and the potential for upstream names to close that gap through selective monetization and portfolio optimization.

Conquer Energy Investing With Authority

In a sector full of noise, Energy Investing Authority from Seeking Alpha Investing Group leader Michael Boyd delivers sharp, high-conviction ideas backed by rigorous on-the-ground research. Led by veteran analyst Michael Boyd, this service goes far beyond the basics, providing institutional-grade energy sector insights you won’t find anywhere else.

Energy Investing Authority gives you access to a full suite of premium tools: Real-time long and short ideas, in-depth company reports, proprietary valuation trackers, and field research from refinery floors to LNG terminals. With a track record that’s outpaced the Russell 2000 by 120% since 2016, this is not just another energy newsletter - it’s a complete intelligence platform.

Stay ahead of commodity cycles. Avoid costly mistakes. And generate sustainable income from companies with real assets and real upside.

Join today for the $799 annual price before it goes up to $999 on June 4.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VSE Corporation: Transition To Aerospace MRO Is Complete
data.description:
In February, I offered some cautionary words on shares of VSE Corporation (NASDAQ:VSEC) after the company appeared to be selling its fleet business on the cheap.

A strategic reset and M&A efforts resulted in the growth accelerating in 2023 and 2024, with margins lagging due to issues in the fleet segment. The company "solved" this by announcing the sale of this business early this year, yet proceeds came in a bit light in my view.

The easy but expensive fix made me a bit cautious as shares still priced in quite some good news. Shares have continued to see healthy gains as the 2025 guidance looks quite decent, while the company announced another bolt-on deal as well, which looks promising.

This comes amidst lofty valuations, all of which makes me a bit cautious here, yet I am inclined to keep a close eye on the shares and M&A path going forward.

MRO & Aftermarket Distribution For Aerospace & Fleet

VSE provides components and services to maximize uptime and lifespan of critical aviation assets of its customers. The company provides so-called MRO services (maintenance, repair and overhaul) for components and engines, with these service provided to commercial, business and general aviation clients.

To understand where the company comes from, I go back to 2023 when the $860 million business reported its results across two segments. The aviation segment, which provides these MRO segments, generated just over half a billion in sales. This was complemented at the time by a >$300 million fleet management business, in which parts and engineering solutions were supplied to medium and heavy-duty markets.

Revenues rose by 29% to $860 million in 2023, on which a solid EBITDA number of $114 million was reported, with adjusted earnings reported at $3.31 per share, as strong growth was supported by a strategic reset initiated in the years before, providing the groundwork for growth to return.

The company guided for continuation of strong growth in 2024, yet there were some moving factors, including a $120 million deal to acquire Turbine Controls, while some smaller assets were sold as well.

After growing first quarter sales by 28% and second quarter sales by as much as 30%, the company announced a $200 million deal for Kellstrom Aerospace in the fall of last year, adding $175 million in sales with EBITDA margins reported around 15%. Following enthusiasm on this deal, shares broke the $100 mark, all while the fleet solutions business saw struggles.

My February Take

In February, VSE announced that it would sell its somewhat troubled fleet business to One Equity Partners in a deal valued at $230 million. That number was quite low, but the structure of the transaction requires some reservations as well, as it only included a $140 million cash component, a $25 million seller note and $65 million contingent earn-out consideration.

Trading around the $100 mark, VSE was valued at $2.5 billion, implying that the total deal (including the earn-out) was valued at less than 10% of the prevailing valuation, while the fleet segment made up 36% of total sales in 2023! While the business was struggling in 2024, it felt like a soft multiple nonetheless.

Later that month, the company reported its 2024 results. Full-year sales rose 26% to $1.08 billion, yet the struggles in the Fleet segment made that adjusted earnings were down 5% to $3.13 per share.

Aviation revenues rose 44% to $786 million as operating margins rose in tandem to $101 million, for margins near 13%. Fleet revenues were down 7% to $294 million as operating earnings were cut in half to $17 million, ahead of allocation of corporate costs.

Net debt was reported at $401 million, for a leverage ratio around 2.5 times, pro forma the Turbine Controls and Kellstrom deal. The company guided for 2025 aviation sales to rise by 35-40%, suggesting $1.06-$1.10 billion in revenues, implying that 2025 sales could come in flat, factoring the divestment of the fleet business.

EBITDA margins of the aviation business are seen around 16%, suggesting EBITDA north of $170 million, as the question is how corporate costs will be absorbed, with activities now only focusing on one segment.

Based on this outlook, shares rose from the $100 mark to nearly $120 per share, as frankly the outlook is quite sound.

Momentum Continues

Despite the concerns in the wider marketplace around tariffs, shares of VSE have seen continued gains, with shares now trading around the $130 mark. On the first day of April, VSE reported that it closed on the sale of its Fleet business.

On the first day of May, VSE announced the acquisition of Turbine Weld Industries, an MRO service provider which focuses on complex engine components. With a $50 million acquisition price, this is a relatively bolt-on deal, although that few financial details on the deal have been announced, other than the purchase price. The first quarter conference call revealed a $20 million revenue contribution, suggesting that a full year price around 2.5 times sales has been paid, with nothing said about margins.

This was followed by the release of the first quarter results early in May, with first quarter sales reported up more than 57% to $256 million and change. The company grew operating income in line with sales, reported at $24 million and change, with GAAP earnings nearly doubling to $0.67 per share, and adjusted earnings coming in at $0.78 per share.

The company ended the quarter with $459 million in net debt, with immediate proceeds from the fleet business seen at $175 million post the quarter, partially offset by a $50 million purchase of Turbine Weld. This resulted in the pro forma net debt being down to $334 million, for a 2.2 times leverage ratio based on EBITDA of $147 million. Note that the business targets leverage around 3 times EBITDA over time, having quite an aggressive operating stance to this.

What Now?

With 20 million and some shares outstanding, and shares having risen to the $103 mark, a $2.69 billion equity valuation makes for a $3.0 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at nearly 3 times sales, about 20 times EBITDA and with adjusted earnings trending over $3 per share, earnings multiples have risen to more than 40 times earnings here.

With the full year adjusted EBITDA guidance seen around $170 million, there is upside in that number, as the one-on-one impact on the bottom line would push adjusted earnings up from about $3 per share, to $4 per share. Even in such a case, earnings multiples are rather demanding, in excess of 30 times.

This is aided by a greater contribution of all dealmaking and recent debt refinancing actions, designed to cut interest expenses, as well as reduction in corporate cost overhead.

data.title: Boliden: A Well-Run European Metals Producer Offers Great Value
data.description:
Boliden (OTCPK:BDNNY) is a Sweden-based miner and metals smelter with facilities scattered across the three Nordic countries (Norway, Sweden and Finland) plus a zinc mine in Northern Ireland. The company is a relatively small player in the mining industry, especially compared with companies like BHP and Glencore, but it punches above its weight in zinc production, coming in as the world’s 12th largest miner of the product. The chart below from Boliden’s 2024 annual report shows the mining segment’s revenues by metal.

Boliden primarily focuses on copper and zinc production, but benefits from ore by-products including gold, silver, nickel and lead. Their mines are heavily automated and highly efficient but produce low-grade ores compared with more productive mines around the world. The company’s existing mining and smelting operations are detailed in the table below.

Facility Type

Name

Description

Metals Produced

Remaining Life (years)

Mines

Aitik

The world’s most productive open-pit copper mine

Primarily copper + some gold/silver

24

Boliden

High-grade mine with high production stability

Gold, zinc, silver, copper, lead, tellurium

9

Garpenberg

The world’s most productive under-ground zinc mine

Zinc, silver, lead, gold, copper

32

Kevitsa

One of Finland’s largest open-pit mines

Copper, nickel, palladium, platinum, gold, cobalt

10

Tara

Europe’s biggest zinc mine

Zinc, lead

6

Smelters

Bergsoe

Europe’s biggest recycler of lead-acid batteries

Recycled lead

N/A

Harjavalta

Europe’s biggest nickel smelter

Copper, nickel in matte, gold, silver, platinum-group metals, sulphuric acid

N/A

Kokkola

Europe’s second biggest zinc producer

Zinc, sulphuric acid, silver

N/A

Odda

One of the world’s most sustainable zinc smelters

Zinc, sulphuric acid

N/A

Ronnskar

One of the world’s most efficient coper smelting plants

Copper, gold, silver, lead, zinc clinker, platinum-group metals, sulphuric acid

N/A

I’ll focus my evaluation on the company’s mining operations before reviewing their competitive position as a smelter.

Copper Mining

Boliden produces copper in their Aitik, Kevitsa and Boliden area mines. The chart below shows the cash cost of their mines compared with others around the world.

Aitik overcomes low-grade ores through a focus on efficiency and advanced automation to come in towards the middle of the cost curve. The Boliden Area mines, however, appear to rank towards the bottom - a dubious position for any metals producer. But this cost curve doesn’t tell the whole story. Boliden (the company) reports costs on a pro-rata basis, which means mining costs are allocated to each metal in proportion to their revenue streams. The Boliden Area mines also produce gold, silver, lead, tellurium and zinc, which together enhance overall profitability. This is illustrated in the Boliden Area mine’s position on the zinc cost curve.

Zinc mining

The zinc chart above is much more charitable to Boliden Area mines. Garpenberg in particular appears to produce zinc at costs below the industry average, though this measure too must be viewed holistically as the mine also produces gold, silver, lead and copper. The Tara mine is positioned unfavorably on the zinc cost curve and does not produce significant amounts of by-product metals, which is why Boliden elected to place the mine under care and maintenance (meaning they shut it down temporarily) when zinc prices crashed in 2023.

This decision came down to both zinc prices and labor relations as evidenced by the fact that Boliden decided to reopen the Northern-Irish mine in 2024 despite a lack of recovery in zinc prices. The decision appears to have been prompted by an agreement with the local labor union. I’ll touch more on labor relations in the “Risk” section of this paper.

Evaluated strictly as a mining company, Boliden leaves much for investors to desire. Europe is sure to value an internal supply of critical metals highly in this turbulent geopolitical age, but owning a pure-play miner towards the upper end of cost curves still seems unwise. Luckily for Boliden, mining is only half of the equation. Let’s look at how the company’s mining operations support the smelting business.

Smelters

Boliden sells the majority of mine output to its own smelters, producing a litany of synergies. By producing 1/3 of the feedstock used by their smelters, Boliden is partially hedged against rising metals costs. It also allows the company to capture profits from occasional surges in metals prices. These volatile earnings from the mining segment are balanced with the steady income provided by the smelting side of the business. Controlling a large portion of feedstock production also reduces transportation and blending costs. Focusing on the smelters – Boliden is able to specifically tailor their smelting operations for the ore that comes from their specific mines. This allows the mines, which might otherwise have to sell their low-grade ores at a discount, to maximize the value of their output. This also helps the smelters optimize by-product recovery since they deal with feedstock from the same mines.

Boliden is slightly more prominent amongst smelters, ranking 7th in zinc smelting worldwide and processing a respectable amount of copper. For reference, their copper smelting capacity is about 1/3 that of giant Glencore. Boliden’s size can be a disadvantage in an industry that relies heavily on economies of scale. However, their location in Europe does confer some advantages. Boliden runs Europe’s biggest lead-acid battery recycling facility in Bergsoe. As the world becomes more geographically fragmented, the company’s smelting capacity should be given a boost from businesses looking to insulate their supply chain against trade disruptions. The Nordic countries in which Boliden operates have abundant hydro and wind powered energy sources, which help the company secure reasonable energy prices. And although the “green” aspect of metals production is becoming less important in America, Europe still places significant weight on carbon emissions. The upcoming European Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) should essentially act as a tariff adjustment which will benefit Boliden’s low-emission metals within European borders.

The company is unique in many ways, most notably their focus on environmentally responsible metals production. Much of their operations have been electrified and automated in a way that reduces carbon emissions, lowers costs, and improves workplace safety. I turn now to the financials to observe how this business model has performed for investors.

Balance Sheet and Returns on Capital

Boliden operates with very low levels of debt. They target a 20% debt/equity ratio but usually find themselves below that line, often operating with 0 net debt. This isn’t uncommon in the mining industry given the volatility of metals prices which have a direct impact on revenues.

Company

Debt/Equity (YE 2024)

Boliden

0.24

Glencore (OTCPK:GLNCY)

0.94

BHP (BHP)

0.08

Rio Tinto (RIO)

0.25

Korea Zinc Group

0.68

Jiangxi Copper Co (OTCPK:JIAXF)

1.05

Freeport-McMoran (FCX)

0.53

Still, it’s good to see that the company has historically been managed in a very conservative manner. The company has historically been very selective about acquisitions (I discuss the recent change to that below), resulting in almost no intangible assets on the balance sheet. At year-end 2024, the company had about $50 worth of tangible equity on the balance sheet, compared with a share price today around $60. The company’s debt has an average term of 3 years and carries a very reasonable 4.3% weighted average interest rate.

Boliden has historically avoided share repurchases, a subject on which I’m a bit torn. On one hand, the company would benefit shareholders by directing excess cash into shares when they’re undervalued. But on the other, I’ve often seen management shoveling cash into their own shares – regardless of price – to artificially push up earnings per share. I’m at least glad to see Boliden’s management avoiding that behavior. A more important measure will be to evaluate how their capital allocation policy has impacted returns on capital.

Return on capital employed (ROCE) is one of my go-to profitability metrics and I’m pleased to see that Boliden’s management also uses it as a guide. In their annual report, the company sets a minimum threshold of 10% ROI on any investment. This has resulted in an average ROCE of 19% over the past 5 years. Take this with a grain of salt, as metal prices have been relatively high over this period which has a direct impact on Boliden’s earnings.

The Recent Acquisition

Acquisitions are a vital part of operating as a commodities producer. Oil companies must purchase leases on new acreage just as mining companies must purchase and develop new mines. One advantage Boliden has is their ability to explore and develop areas around existing mines, which can reduce time to production and exploration costs. However, in December 2024 Boliden decided to purchase two new mines outside of their existing operations, the Neves-Corvo and Zinkgruvan mines, for about $1.45 billion. While the Zinkgruvan mine will expand Boliden’s operations in their home country of Sweden, the Neves-Corvo mine is located in Portugal, which is outside of their traditional operating area. The transaction will expand zinc production from 35% to 70% of smelter capacity and from 30% to 40% for copper smelting. Zinkgruvan’s cash cost position is in the middle of the second quartile of the zinc mining cost curve and Neves-Corvo is in the 75th percentile of the cost curve for both zinc and copper. Management operates the mining and smelting businesses separately and said on the Q4, 2024 earnings call that there was no intention to become fully integrated, with the smelters 100% supplied from their own mines.

Boliden has said that they expect to earn $300 million to $350 million in EBITDA annually from these newly acquired mines over the next five years. Boliden’s average capex across all mines over the past 10 years has been about 55% of EBITDA. Subtracting the investment required to operate and explore new territory around existing mines, EBIT from this acquisition may be estimated at roughly $150 million, resulting in a pre-tax return around 10%. This leaves out the possibility of future earnings growth from expanded production, greater efficiency, or additional reserves that Boliden may be able to unearth. Boliden’s CEO, Mikael Staffas, seemed to indicate those opportunities played into their purchase decision during the Q1, 2025 earnings call.

just to be very clear, the big important part here, how to create value is around how to make sure that we increase the life length of these mines. They are, as you know, today, nine-year assets, both of them. So exploration will be an important part, but also how we work on productivity

All things considered, this seems to be a reasonable acquisition completed at a fair price. My only qualm with the decision lies with how they’re planning on funding the acquisition, issuing some debt, eliminating the dividend and issuing additional equity. As a shareholder myself, I’m actually content with management’s decision to temporarily cancel the dividend to help fund this purchase. Eliminating the dividend to avoid issuing more share or taking on additional debt is a sound decision. But management also plans to issue shares to fund 25% of this acquisition – a decision I’m less thrilled with. During the Q1, 2025 earnings call an analyst pointed out that it didn’t seem like they needed to issue shares given the available liquidity on the balance sheet. The company’s CFO, Håkan Gabrielsson, responded:

No, I think it's of course when we model things and when we model things also in downturns, we need to make sure that we don't have it too stretched balance sheet and we have very clear targets around that. And, if things move along according to our plans, there is still a little bit of a gap to where we want to be in the terms of the strength of the balance sheet.

The share issue, which was completed on March 21, raised the share count by 10.7 million shares to 284.2 million shares, an increase of 3.9%. I agree with the analyst that a share issuance seems unnecessary. But the increase in share count is relatively minor and I’m comforted somewhat by management’s risk averse posture.

Earnings Power

Boliden’s revenues are directly impacted by volatile metal prices, which makes estimating their mid-cycle earnings power a tricky exercise. To do so, I’ve quantified historical production levels from each mine and priced their output at average metals prices over the past decade, which should help temper temporary swings in production and prices. Concerning the smelters, whose revenues tend to be more consistent from year to year, I’ve looked at average EBITDA margins and capex as a percent of revenues to determine an average level of pre-tax earnings. This analysis produced the following estimation of Boliden’s current earnings power.

Measure

Value (millions of SEK except per-share data)

Mine earnings

4,372

Smelter earnings

7,752

Acquisition earnings

1,419

Boliden EBIT

9,171

Interest

679

Tax (20%)

1,698

Boliden mid-cycle owner earnings

6,793

Shares outstanding

284 million shares

EPS

23.90 SEK

EPS (OTC conversion)

$4.45/share

The “earnings” figures from mines, smelters and the acquisition are my estimation of EBITDA minus the investment required to operate and expand each resource. This estimate incorporates some conservatism by including growth capex as an expense rather than an investment. This may cause my estimate to be too conservative, but it gives me some comfort that I'm adding to my margin of safety.

Earnings growth will depend heavily on metals prices, but Boliden is making significant investments in smelters to expand capacity in response to rising metals demand in Europe. It would be silly to estimate “earnings growth” for a mining company like Boliden, but I’d be happy to pick up additional shares towards the bottom of the range the OTC shares have been trading in over the past two years – between $50 and $70. Shares fell to about $55 during the recent tariff scare, presenting a good purchase opportunity.

Management

I think management is doing a fantastic job overall. While I wasn’t thrilled with the recent share issue, a tendency towards conservatism will serve long-term shareholders well. As I’ve studied the company, I’ve been impressed with a few policies that aren’t in line with the institutional standard which so often displaces thought, particularly in large American businesses.

The first indication of great management comes in the form of a risk mitigation tool which the company chooses to forgo: hedging with derivatives. In the company’s annual report they state:

The Group policy is not to hedge metal prices, but rather to allow changes to be reflected in profits. However, we hedge smelter metal price exposure of payable metals in the period between the purchase of the raw material and the sale of the corresponding metal.

I love this policy. While it seems to be fairly common amongst most large-scale miners, I don’t get the impression that Boliden’s management feels the need to manage earnings from year to year.

I also like that they’ve eliminated their dividend this year to pay for the acquisition. If the business were privately owned by its managers, this is likely how they would choose to fund the transaction. After all, why would they take money out of the business just to put more back in to fund growth? Management clearly thinks as an owner, and this is partly because they are owners. CEO Mikael Staffas has twice made large open market purchases of the shares over the past 2 years when the price has fallen below $60/otc share.

Management operates the business using ROCE as a guide rather than any type of sales growth. Investments are clearly scrutinized to make sure they exceed a 10% ROI threshold, ensuring a satisfactory return on retained shareholder capital. This attitude comes through in management comments on earnings calls. More importantly, their words are backed up through action.

Looking forward

Boliden celebrated their 100 year anniversary in 2024, marking the moment when gold was first discovered near current Boliden Area mines in 1924. The company enters their second century in a very healthy position with increasing demand for the metals they produce. If the company struggles in the future, it won’t be from lack of demand.

Few expect metals demand to go anywhere but up for the foreseeable future, but this is only half the equation. As the saying goes – the cure for high prices is high prices, and vice versa. If metals prices continue to rise, people will find alternatives and supply will rise to meet that demand. If prices fall, suspended mining and newly economic uses will slowly bring demand into line. This supply/demand dynamic produces an environment in which a conservatively-managed mining operation such as Boliden’s can produce solid earnings over time.

The smelting side of the business has characteristics that may help it differentiate its product to a degree. I mentioned previously the importance of Boliden’s geographic location, with all smelters located within the European Economic Area. In an increasingly fragmented world, European customers should be increasingly willing to pay for metals supply with lower supply chain risk. Smelters in the Nordic countries are particularly well-placed to provide this supply with abundant renewable energy, supportive governments, and low shipping rates with direct access to the Baltic Sea. As usage of electronics continues to rise, Boliden’s recycling facilities will process increasing levels of feedstock. This will be an increasingly important source of metals for the world going forward, especially in climate conscious Europe.

Current capital projects give a good illustration of how Boliden will expand incrementally moving forward. The company is investing in an expansion of the zinc smelter at Odda which will expand annual production volumes from 200 to 350 ktonnes once complete. Their other large ongoing project is an expanded tankhouse in Ronnskar. This project was prompted by a fire at their previous tankhouse in June 2023. Boliden received insurance proceeds to pay for this incident (which has somewhat inflated 2024 earnings figures) but has used it as an opportunity to improve the facility and increase copper cathode production.

Risks

The biggest risk to Boliden shareholders is metals prices. Thanks to their conservatively managed finances, a price drop shouldn't threaten Boliden as a going concern. But it would be certain to reduce revenues and earnings per share. The price of the metals Boliden sells have been going up in aggregate for a period of over 10 years now. The chart below shows a weighted average index made up of currency and metals prices that affect Boiden's revenues.

This has helped increase Boliden’s earnings over the past decade even though production has remained flat. If the trend were to go into reverse, as it eventually will, Boliden’s earnings will decline along with it.

Another risk I’d like to highlight for Boliden revolves around labor issues. I mentioned earlier that it took an amended compensation agreement with employees at the Tara zinc mine to get operations running again. Boliden also experienced strikes in their Finland-based operations in 2024. Staffas commented on this issue during the Q4, 2024 earnings call:

And then we have strikes in Finland. This situation is not resolved. We do not have a collective bargaining agreement on national level in place yet but what we've seen so far from strike actions in Q1 has roughly affected us by 100 million Swedish Krona negatively. As of right now, there is no strike action ongoing and the negotiations have resumed, which we feel good about. Hopefully we'll also be able to find a solution to this situation. And just to be very clear, this is no specific Boliden, this is very much part of the national negotiations in Finland on the collective bargaining side.

As Staffas mentioned, these strikes are not specific to Boliden. But they do operate in Nordic countries with high incomes and generous social safety nets. This expensive source of labor saps competitiveness against low-cost producers in places like Indonesia and Africa, which may help explain part of Boliden’s commitment to mine automation.

The next risk to address is energy prices. Though Boliden has been able to source reasonably priced energy thus far, Europe continues to push through a very expensive transition to cleaner energy. Wind is indeed plentiful nearby in the North Sea, but that doesn’t mean the wind turbines deployed to capture it are particularly cheap. If Europe continues retiring dispatchable coal and gas plants without replacing them with dispatchable generation in the form of nuclear, power prices could rise to hurt Boliden’s competitiveness.

The final risk I’ll mention for Boliden shareholders is environmental regulation. If public concern rises over the company’s effect on the environment, it may impede expansion plans or even force operations to move elsewhere. For example, Boliden applied for a mining concession for their Laver project all the way back in 2014. This application has repeatedly been rejected and is still going through litigation today – 10 years later!

Summary

Boliden is a relatively small producer of metals, especially compared with giant companies like BHP and Glencore. But this unique company operates some of the most efficient and environmentally friendly mining and smelting facilities in the world. The company has produced positive earnings in each of the past 25 years and is run by a conservative management team that behaves like owners. The volatility of metals prices will continue to be mirrored in the company's shares. Long-term investors that see the fundamental value of this conservatively managed company should use this to their advantage and purchase additional shares when the price of otc shares fall below $60.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brink's: A Mixed History With Signs Of Stability
data.description:
Company Description

The Brink's Company (NYSE:BCO) is a global provider of secure logistics for cash and valuables. It also offers digital retail solutions as well as services for ATMs. BCO employs more than 67,000 people, and has operations in over 100 countries. The company sports a market cap of about $4B, and over the past 10 years, it has a total return of a little more than 225%, resulting in a CAGR of 12.6%, essentially in-line with the broad market.

Quality Financial Metrics

As I'm sure you are aware, I typically examine three financial metrics to determine if a business is of high quality. It's important we see increasing revenue, a steady gross profit margin, and a healthy return on invested capital. Let's review these metrics for BCO and see how they measure up.

The revenue per share for BCO has generally trended higher, aside from FY16 and FY20, when the RPS shrank slightly. From 2017 through 2019, the RPS grew from $66 to $73.4 a share, achieving a CAGR of about 5.5% per year. Then Covid hit in 2020, and as previously mentioned, revenue was basically flat. Post Covid, this metric grew at a 10% clip, although the increases have been slowing. The revenue per share rose by almost 16% in FY21, but last year it increased by only 7%, still respectable, but a noticeable slowdown.

The gross profit margin has expanded nicely over the past decade. In 2015, the GPM was 20.2% and by 2019, it had risen to more than 23%. There was a slight decline in 2020 due to Covid, but every year since this metric has improved, surpassing 25% last year.

While the first two metrics are clearly trending in a positive direction, the return on invested capital metric has struggled. In 2015 & 2018 the company had a negative ROIC; however, BCO operates in a capital-intensive industry where efficiency is critical. Those two fiscal years were transitional for Brinks, during which they incurred restructuring costs, faced some operational inefficiencies, and made acquisitions, resulting in net losses of $11.9M and $33.3M, respectively. Although I'm typically looking for a ROIC closer to the mid-teens or even 20%, that doesn't seem realistic for BCO in the near future.

Dividend Data

Brink's dividend growth has been inconsistent at best. In fact, prior to 2017, BCO hadn't raised its dividend since 2007. Following the dividend hike in 2017, it wasn't until 2021 before BCO announced another dividend increase, raising it from $0.15 to $0.20 per quarter. Brinks also skipped their "typical" May dividend increase in FY22, but has since raised its dividend each of the last three years, including a recent 5% dividend increase announcement. As shown by the orange line, prior to FY21 the payout ratio was highly volatile prior to FY21 but has somewhat stabilized. Ideally, investors will see more consistent dividend increases in the coming years, but for now, it's something to monitor.

Q1 2025 Earnings

On Monday, May 12th, Brink's announced their Q1 2025 earnings, GAAP EPS came up $0.01 short of expectations at $1.19 per share, while Non-GAAP EPS was $1.62 per share and topped estimates by $0.45. Additionally, revenue for the quarter was $1.25B, exceeding the analyst consensus by more than $33M.

Looking at the revenue by segment, the North America segment saw revenue increase 3% to $418M, with adjusted EBITDA coming in at $73M, resulting in a margin of 17.4%, up from 16.9% in Q1 2024. The primary driver of these results was price increases, though productivity initiatives also contributed.

The Latin America segment struggled, with revenue down 8% YoY, and adjusted EBITDA following suit, declining 14% to $67M. This segment faced challenges as a result of unfavorable exchange rates, mainly the Mexican Peso, Argentine Peso, as well as the Brazilian Real.

The Europe segment generated revenue of nearly $300M; however, EBITDA remained flat, with the margin shrinking minimally. Revenue increased in this segment, driven by mostly organic growth, though it was slightly offset by unfavorable currency exchange rates.

Lastly, the Rest of the World segment saw the strongest performance across all metrics. Revenue grew 9% to $222M from $205M, while EBITDA rose 20% YoY to $57M. Revenue increased organically by $19M, or 9% but was partially offset by weak exchange rates.

Management also provided guidance for Q2, which can be seen below.

The earnings release was not received well by the market, with the stock declining 5%, and for the week it is down about 7.5%.

The entire earnings release can be read/listened to here.

Valuation

Let's review Brink's valuation from a free cash flow perspective and decide if the company is trading for an attractive valuation. Before diving into that, let me add a little more context on how to view the chart below. The estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and lastly, the Trim line is 40% above the calculated FV.

The custom valuation model suggests the stock is trading for a significant premium; however, the fluctuation in free cash flow has made it difficult to properly value the company. Over the past half decade the FCF was flat from 2021 to 2022 with both years around $6.30 per share. In 2023, the FCF jumped to nearly $11 per share, but in 2024 it was more than cut in half.

The long-term expected rate of return currently sits at 7.9%, moderately below my threshold of 10%. This estimate comprises three factors, a forward dividend yield of 1.11%, a return to fair value factor of -11.71% and an expected earnings growth rate of 18.5%.

In summary, BCO's total return has kept up with the overall market over the past decade. The financials for Brink's are decent, with the RPS and GPM margin both trending in the right direction. The dividend history has been inconsistent, but I believe the company striving to establish itself as a reliable dividend growth stock, especially since the recent earnings report highlighted that the latest increase marked the 3rd consecutive year. Based on the valuation model above, the stock appears to be overvalued, and I believe investors would be best served waiting for a larger pullback or a significant increase in FCF before initiating or expanding their position.

data.title: Prologis: The Warehouse King With A 3.8% Yield And Room To Run
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Having covered a semiconductor ETF recently, I sort of wanted to keep the vibe going, and in doing so, I went with another ETF angle, but on the industrial side, in Prologis (PLD). Like with the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH), Prologis is facing similar choppiness in the stock market, juggling a bunch of headwinds just like SMH is. However, with somewhat clarity around the tariff situation, we’ve seen healthy near-term momentum in both stocks. Additionally, Prologis has done well to tactfully navigate the situation through its build-to-suit discipline, while capitalizing on its diversified client base. Also, despite the recent run-up, the stock’s still sitting roughly 15% behind its intrinsic value (based on my dividend discount model (DDM)), while offering a multi-year dividend yield at 3.8%. Summing it up, at current prices, Prologis appears to be a high-conviction, “buy on weakness” opportunity.

Prologis In Review

Prologis is the biggest name in the industrial REIT space, and has everything to do with warehouses, distribution centers, and logistics-related real estate. Put simply, it builds and owns massive fulfillment centers and storage spaces that the big companies use for stashing and shipping their stuff.

Its portfolio is huge, spanning upwards of 1.3 billion square feet, with about 95% of that being occupied. What’s more, its client base includes the who's who of e-commerce, retail, and manufacturing. To elaborate on that, 39% of its tenants are companies targeting essentials, a space where demand expands in line with population growth. 31% of its tenants are involved in cyclical business, where customer confidence is high, but things can get rough in a downturn situation. The remaining 30% benefits from long-term secular trends, particularly in online retail. E-commerce is arguably Prologis’s biggest long-term growth driver, and its end-to-end setup gives it a major competitive edge in the space with some serious staying power.

Recent Financial Performance

Prologis delivered a relatively solid Q1 performance, spearheaded by an impressive bottom-line showing. Funds From Operations [FFO] came in at $1.42, topping estimates by four cents, marking an 11% bump from the previous year. The beat on its FFO is perhaps even more impressive, considering yet another top-line miss (by $5.15 million). Overall, though, the results highlight its resilient cash-generating prowess, marked by almost a 50% free cash flow margin.

On the business side, things were mostly exceptional. It signed leases for a massive 58 million square feet of space during the quarter, with an average occupancy of close to 95%. What’s more, its tenant retention was around 73%, highlighting low churn and strong stickiness with Prologis.

On top of that, the REIT’s net effective rent change came in at a superb 53.7% on new and renewed leases (32.1% on a cash basis). In other words, once a lease expired for a tenant, the new one was approximately one-third higher on a cash basis. To add to that, same-store net operating income was around 6.2% higher on a YOY basis, showing that existing properties are generating a lot more income than a year ago.

Nonetheless, the big news with Prologis on the financial side was its decision not to raise guidance despite the bottom-line Q1 beat. A big reason for that was the lack of clarity around the macroeconomic situation, especially with the tariff battles at this time. As a consequence, 78% of the new developments undertaken during Q1 were built-to-suit, meaning they already have tenants lined up. Clients are mostly on the fence over committing to risky, speculative builds at this point, favoring short-term, flexible storage solutions, or perhaps using existing space more efficiently.

To put it all together, Prologis’s operating performance is mostly impressive while it maintains a healthy financial positioning ($6.5 billion in total liquidity).

A Look At Its Dividend Profile

Prologis’s dividend profile has now become a lot more attractive, thanks to the meaningful pullback in its share price.

It currently yields 3.8%, which soars above what it was yielding back in 2020 at around 2.3%. The shift effectively highlights a steady cadence in annual dividend hikes, along with a stock price that’s considerably behind its peak. Looking further into its history, the company’s year-end payouts surged from 1.5% in 2021 to 2.8% the following year, eventually peaking at 3.6% last year. In all likelihood, its average yield, currently hovering around 3.5%, should finish the year noticeably higher than last year.

On top of that, the stock’s dividend-growth metrics earn top honors, with a 9% trailing TTM growth rate (roughly 275% higher than the REIT sector median). Zooming out over a three-year horizon, Prologis still delivers a punch with a 13.2% CAGR in dividends, with a five-year pace clocking in at a superb 12.4%.

Valuation Discussion

Prologis stock has been mostly volatile of late, currently trading near the low $100s, over 15% behind its 2021 highs.

Following the pandemic-era boom, the stock’s been taking a beating on the back of interest rate hikes and recession fears. Though it recovered well during the AI-led rally, it’s now back in the grinder, with things looking remarkably choppy. Tariff headwinds and Prologis’s cautious outlook have just rubbed more salt in investors’ wounds.

Despite the pullback, though, the snapshot from Seeking Alpha shows that the REIT’s stock price isn’t all that cheap.

For instance, on a TTM basis, it's trading at 27 times earnings, just shy of the 27.3x median. On a forward basis, the trend gets even worse, where it trades 20% above the sector average. Also, it's trading at over 11 times sales, which is triple the sector median, and roughly 20x is operating cash flows.

To investigate further, I built a three-stage DDM, which points to a healthy upside from current levels. The model starts off with consensus dividend projections of $4.05 in 2025, $4.29 in 2026, and $4.55 in 2027, while assuming a conservative 4% perpetual growth rate. Thereafter, we discount those cash flows with nearly a 7% required return (the long-term, inflation-adjusted S&P 500 average). All told, the model delivers an intrinsic value of close to $124.20.

With PLD trading close to $108 at the time of writing, that amounts to a double-digit bump from current levels.

Risks To The Thesis

Though Prologis looks mostly compelling at current prices, there are a few risks worth considering that potentially challenge its near- and medium-term outlook. For starters, if things went south on the economic front (as the past few months have shown), we could see a meaningful pullback in consumer spending, forcing tenants to potentially downsize or default. Truth be told, though, the company’s diversified, investment-grade tenant base offers a great deal of insulation on that front. Similarly, if we see interest rates drift higher, that will most likely put considerable strain on Prologis’s liquidity position, while making its dividend yield a lot less attractive against bonds. Finally, despite a broad tenant roster, a handful of large customers like Amazon (AMZN) still carry a ton of concentration risk.

Bottom-Line On Prologis Stock

On balance, Prologis offers an attractive buy‐on‐weakness opportunity, backed by a multi-year-high dividend yield. Moreover, its operational end looks mostly solid, with healthy occupancy rates, rent expansion, and an eye-catching dividend profile. Hence, for investors looking to scoop up a stock with meaningful upside and compelling income potential, look no further than PLD.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Is Credit At A Turning Point?
data.description:
Some market downturns are triggered by exogenous events that were unforeseen, often called Black Swans. The correction of April 3, 2025, can't be placed in that category. President Trump's "liberation day", when the US announced a 10% universal tariff on all countries and a range of "reciprocal" tariffs on a range of countries (ranging from 10%-50%), was widely telegraphed, not least by the President himself.

So why did risk assets - including credit - go into reverse? The breadth and depth of the tariffs went beyond most expectations. The US will now have an effective tariff rate of 22.4% (Yale Budget Lab), higher than the Smoot-Hawley level in the 1930s and not seen since the first decade of the 20th century.

A new era of protectionism appears to be upon us. Is this the catalyst for a decisive shift in the credit cycle? The movement in CDS indices indicates a material change in sentiment. S&P DJI's iTraxx and CDX indices - tradable products that are highly liquid and excellent barometers of credit risk - widened significantly. The CDX IG index moved nearly 5bps to 65.5bps, its widest level since November 2023. While this daily move is not in the same bracket seen during the extreme volatility of 2020, it is still in the top 3% (excluding index roll dates) since the beginning of 2020.

The equivalent index in Europe, the iTraxx Main, widened by a similar amount to trade at 68.5bps. This needs to be placed in recent context. During the post-US election euphoria, when US exceptionalism was at its height, the CDX IG was trading in the 47-50bps range, a full 10bps tighter than the iTraxx Main. They converged as risk aversion rose in North America with the initial tariff announcements and the EU - particularly Germany - promised a Keynesian fiscal injection.

Investment-grade credit markets went into 2025 with strong technical tailwinds. Supply of paper - though robust - was outstripped by demand due to an abundance of capital. All-in yields were relatively high and attractive to investors. Spreads were historically tight in the bond market and also in the synthetic market; the long run median of the CDX IG is 70bps.

Despite the pervading uncertainty - likely to be the word of the year - the index is still below this average level. Bulls in IG credit will point to the strong fundamentals (leverage and interest coverage), balance sheet discipline and the willingness and capability of BBB credits to maintain their IG ratings. But the risks are clear: EBITDA could suffer if a trade war causes a marked reduction in growth and the technical bid fades in strength.

These risks are perhaps even more pertinent to high-yield. The CDX HY index is now trading at 405bps, 24bps wider on the day and well over 100bps north of its level in December. This is a significant move. At the beginning of the year, it was trading at the same level as the iTraxx Crossover - now it is 58bps wider. Credit quality is poorer in North American HY compared to Europe, and the recent moves reflect investors reducing risk at the weaker end of the credit spectrum.

The consensus view going into 2025 was that spreads would continue to tighten, supported by strong demand, robust fundamentals and a soft landing in the economy. That view, while understandable and still possible, now looks less feasible. Much will depend on the extent and duration of the tariff programme - will elements get reversed and how will the rest of the world respond? The policy decisions of the Fed and other central banks will also be crucial.

But this new regime will also present opportunities to credit investors. Dispersion was already on the rise, and the impact of tariffs will mean some sectors and individual names will lose out more than others. National governments will react in different ways. Credit selection will be paramount as well as prudent risk management. Instruments such as the CDS indices and CDS index options (the best hedge of credit volatility) may gain in popularity (the chart above shows trading volumes rising in CDX ahead of the tariff announcement). If complacency was evident in credit last year, there is surely no room for that in 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Acadian Timber Corp: Tariff Uncertainty & Potential Topping Pattern Warrant Investor Caution
data.description:
Intro

Canadian outfit Acadian Timber Corp. (OTCPK:ACAZF) is a supplier of forest-oriented products in eastern Canada and the northeastern US. The company just reported its first-quarter earnings numbers for the present fiscal year (Earnings released 10/5/2025), where GAAP profits of CAD$3.7 million (CAD$0.21 per share) trailed the CAD$6 million bottom-line figure (CAD$0.35 per share) in the same period of 12 months prior. Dividends of CAD$0.29 per share were paid out in the quarter, which means that earnings as we stand have not covered the dividend in Q1 and over a trailing 12-month basis. The above may surprise investors given the double-digit top-line growth rate in fiscal 2024. Acadian's generous dividend yield now stands at approximately 6.7%. Given the sustained uptrend in the stock for some time now, the above-average dividend remains the principal calling card in this play at this time.

In fact, if we go to the stock's long-term technicals, we see that the multi-year trend line, which commenced in 2009, remains intact. Furthermore, given the consolidation in the stock in recent months, shares are trading pretty close to downside support, signalling a potential long-entry point on any successfully tested down move.

However, as chartists, we believe that all of Acadian's known fundamentals have already been digested by the resulting share-price action on the technical chart. This means Acadian's the ramifications of voluntary carbon credit sales and real-estate initiatives have been embedded into the share-price action at this stage. The absence of a positive GAAP dividend payout ratio is concerning as it brings into question whether the dividend (at its current level) can be maintained.

Long-Term Technicals

The chart depicts shares of Acadian not being able to break above long-term resistance in the latter stages of last year. While there is nothing inherently wrong with this (as stocks often need time to punch above resistance and begin reporting higher highs), what is apparent is that Acadian's long-term up move has been weakening since 2021. This is confirmed by the divergence in the long-term RSI indicator. This leads us to believe that a downside test of support is more likely over the near term rather than a breakout to all-time highs.

lntermediate Technicals

When we go to a 5-year chart and depict a bullish trendline dating back to 2020, we see that technical damage was inflicted in ACAZF early last month when shares dropped to $11.50 a share. Furthermore, the loss of momentum is more evident on this chart, which is why we most likely are dealing with a topping pattern here instead of a bullish ascending triangle. Therefore, although dividend investors like to compound their gains by investing aggressively at lower prices, this may not be the right course of action to take at the present moment in time.

Return On Equity Conundrum

Return on equity measures the profitability of a company compared to its equity. Acadian's stated return on equity comes in at 5.85% over a trailing twelve month average. ROE is an excellent profitability metric, especially when compared to competitors and also against the respective company's long-term mean. As we see below. Acadian's ROE, while only slightly beating the sector median (5.47%), trails the company's 5-year average (8.6%) by some distance.

This means that despite the strong gains Acadian has reported over the past five years, the company has made fewer profits off its equity on a percentage basis. This has ramifications for the company in a rising bond yield environment. Let me explain.

When bond yields rise, a company's cost of equity invariably becomes more expensive. Remember, the cost of equity is essentially the return shareholders require to invest in the stock. Therefore, the higher long-term fixed investments go, the higher return shareholders will demand, all things remaining equal. We calculate Acadian's cost of equity through the following formula

Cost of equity = Risk-Free Rate + (Beta) x (Equity Risk Premium)

Using the 10-year US bond yield as our risk-free rate and the third-party Damodaran number for our equity risk premium

Cost of equity = 4.52% + (0.64)(4.33%) = 7.29%

Therefore, since Acadian's COE comes in higher than its ROE, this raises serious concerns about whether the company can create sustainable shareholder value going forward.

Uncertainty Not Helping Investor Sentiment

Given how tariffs will play out in North America continues to remain uncertain, it will be interesting to see whether top-line revenues can improve on the CAD$116 million reported last year. Then, as mentioned, you have rising long-term interest rates, which have the potential to put a damper on investor demand over time.

Climate change affects Acadian in a big way, in that any unforeseen events have the potential to result in extensive trucking delays, which again can affect the financials. Remember, to increase the company's return on capital, Acadian needs to be able to turn over its capital as quickly as possible. Foul weather conditions can act as a deterrent here from a delivery success standpoint.

Softwood sawlog end-markets have been struggling with pricing, having been adversely affected as a result. The Hardwood sawlog space has a different issue (supply constraints), so this should put upward pressure on pricing over time. On the pulpwood side concerning both softwood & hardwood, management relayed on the recent Q1 earnings call that demand will most likely remain constrained, all things remaining equal.

Demand and pricing for softwood pulpwood is expected to remain at reduced levels in the near term and demand for hardwood pulpwood begin to soften due to elevated regional inventories as we exited the quarter which may affect demand and pricing in the near term, but we expect volumes and pricing to improve as we progress through the remainder of the year as this inventory is depleted.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we believe the right rating for Acadian Timber is a 'Hold' at present. Profitability concerns and ongoing uncertainty in end-markets should be enough for investors to warrant a cautious stance at this juncture. We look forward to continued coverage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
